title: The Carbon Cycle
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive simulation showing carbon fluxes between atmosphere, ocean, terrestrial biosphere, and lithosphere with adjustable fossil fuel, deforestation, and ocean temperature parameters

learningObjectives: Distinguish fast and slow carbon fluxes and predict how changes in one flux affect atmospheric CO2
Analyze cause-and-effect relationships within the carbon cycle system
Evaluate the relative magnitude of natural vs. anthropogenic carbon fluxes
prerequisites: Basic understanding of photosynthesis and cellular respiration
Familiarity with chemical reservoirs and fluxes

keyQuestions: What happens to atmospheric CO2 when fossil fuel burning doubles?
Why does deforestation increase atmospheric CO2 even without burning fossil fuels?
How does ocean temperature affect the carbon cycle?
What is the difference between a carbon source and a carbon sink?
Why is the carbon cycle considered a system with feedback loops?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think all carbon fluxes operate on the same timescale, when geological processes are millions of times slower than biological ones
Many students believe the ocean is an unlimited carbon sink that can absorb any amount of CO2
Students may not realize that deforestation removes both a carbon sink and releases stored carbon
assessmentOpportunities: Students predict outcomes before adjusting sliders, then compare predictions to observations
Students explain the feedback loop between ocean warming and reduced CO2 absorption
Students design an experiment using the simulation to compare mitigation strategies
